content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Charles Lester 
4545 Cherryvale A venue 
Soquel, CA 95073 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-047 
Dear Mr. Lester: 
March 17, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we provide advice under the post-government employment provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest, Section 1090, or Public Contract Code Section 10411. Additionally, we are not a finder of 
fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide 
assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
As the retired Executive Director and Climate Change Program Manager of the California 
Coastal Commission ("Commission"), are you prohibited under the "permanent ban" from 
performing consulting work for a third party representing the City of Imperial Beach or the City of 
Pacific Grove related to updating each city's Local Coastal Program ("LCP")? 
CONCLUSION 
Given the facts presented, the permanent ban does not bar your participation in advising a 
contract consultant to the City of Imperial Beach or the City of Pacific Grove, so long as the LCP 
amendments at issue do not constitute a continuation of earlier proceedings in which you 
participated in more than an oversight role. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Tille 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-047 
Page No. 2 
You were employed with the California Coastal Commission from June, 2, 1997 to 
September 14, 2016, at which time you retired from state service. During this time, you served in 
the Santa Cruz district office (1997 to 2011), as the Executive Director of the Commission from 
(2011 to February 2016) and as the Commission's Climate Change Program Manager (March to 
September 2016). 
You wish to work as an independent contractor consultant for a third party contractor to the 
City of Imperial Beach on the project of updating the city's existing Local Coastal Program 
("LCP"). The city is proposing to update its LCP pursuant to a planning grant requested by the city 
in March 2016 and awarded by the Coastal Commission in August of 2016. The city has issued a 
request for proposals to do the LCP amendment planning work. 
You also wish to work as a consultant for a third party contractor to the City of Pacific 
Grove regarding its LCP update. This city does not currently have a certified LCP, and seeks 
Commission certification of its updated Land Use Plan ("LUP") and proposed Implementation Plan 
("IP"). You anticipate providing policy review and advice on the proposed LCP to the third party 
contractor. The City of Pacific Grove was awarded a grant to perform this work in January 2014, 
while you were the director of the Commission. 
The Commission is an independent, quasi-judicial state agency, charged with regulatory 
oversight of land use and public access within the "coastal zone." LCPs derive from a requirement 
of the California Coastal Act, the authorizing statute of the Commission. The Commission reviews 
and approves the LCPs, and any amendments, of local governments in the coastal zone. LCP's once 
certified by the Commission, enable the local governmental entity to take over the responsibility for 
issuing coastal development permits. The LCPs require updates, in the form of amendments, to 
address environmental, legal and political changes in the area. These updates may address changes 
in city goals, climate change, or housing density. The LCP updates must be reviewed and approved 
by the Commission. The Commission instituted a local assistance grant program several years ago 
to support local governments in completing or updating their LCPs. 
As Executive Director of the Coastal Commission, you did not play a direct role in 
supervising the processing of LCP update grant applications by cities. This task was performed by 
staff analysts who coordinated with Commission district staff. You did participate in the formation, 
budgeting and design of the overall grant program, and provided general oversight of the 
implementation of the program. A deputy director was in charge of the grant program 
implementation, over whom you had oversight. Part of your duties as Executive Director included 
making recommendations to the Commission regarding regulatory actions on permit applications, 
local coastal program submittals, enforcement actions, and a broad range of other matters. These 
recommendations are based on independent staff analysis. 
You state that each city's proposed LCP update is a new and distinct LCP amendment 
submittal to the Commission, subject to specific review requirements under the Coastal Act and its 
implementing regulations. 


File No. A-17-047 
Page No. 3 
During your time as Executive Director you worked, broadly, on matters submitted by the 
City of Imperial Beach to the Commission. You state that the City oflmperial Beach's original 
certification occurred in 1984, with an update in 1994. Several specific amendments to the LCP 
occurred during the time you served as Executive Director. Your role in these amendments 
consisted of high-level review of the status of the LCP work with the Deputy Director overseeing 
the city's district. In regard to the City oflmperial Beach's current effort to update its LCP 
pursuant to the planning grant awarded by the Coastal Commission in August of 2016, you state 
that you did not participate in the review, recommendation or award of the grant, as this grant 
process occurred after March, 2016 and after your service as the Executive Director ended. 
As to the City of Pacific Grove, you state that your involvement in this LCP update process 
also consisted of high-level review of the status of the LCP work with the Deputy Director 
overseeing the city's district. You believe that the Commission staff's first formal written 
comments to the City of Pacific Grove occurred after you were no longer Executive Director. You 
did not participate in these comments. The city was awarded its LCP grant by the Commission in 
2014, however, your involvement was one of general oversight on the implementation of the grant 
program. The city anticipates submitting its LCP amendment to the Commission for formal review 
in the next several months. The submittal will trigger the Commission's formal process of 
reviewing the amendment. 
Additionally, you do not believe that any specific issues, decision, or projects that you 
supervised as Climate Change Program Manager will be revisited in either city's LCP. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act: the one-year ban and the permanent ban. These provisions 
are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions. (Sections 87400-87406 and 
Regulation 18746.1.) 
One-Year Ban 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal appearance or any oral or written communication, before his or her former 
agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary 
act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or 
contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1.) 
You are subject to the one-year ban until September 2017 and you may not make a formal or 
informal appearance before the Commission on behalf of a client, for the purpose of influencing the 
amendment or award of an LCP update or certification, during that time. You may provide policy 
review and advice on the proposed LCP to a third party contractor, if you are not identified with 
these efforts and so long as you are not otherwise prohibited from such representation under the 
permanent ban. 


Permanent Ban 
File No. A-17-047 
Page No. 4 
The "pennanent ban" prohibits the representation for compensation of any person other than 
the State of California, in any proceeding you participated in as a public official. Section 87401 
states: 
No fonner state administrative official, after the termination of his or her 
employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, 
or otherwise represent, any other person (other than the State of California) before 
any court or state administrative agency or any officer or employee thereof by 
making any fonnal or infonnal appearance, or by making any oral or written 
communication with the intent to influence, in connection with any judicial, 
quasi-judicial or other proceeding if both of the following apply: 
(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial 
interest. 
(b) The proceeding is one in which the fonner state administrative official 
participated. 
Section 87402 applies this ban to assisting others in such representation: 
No fonner state administrative official, after the tennination of his or her 
employment or tenn of office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel, consult 
or assist in representing any other person (except the State of California) in any 
proceeding in which the official would be prohibited from appearing under 
Section 87401 
As the former Executive Director of the Commission, you are subject to the pennanent ban 
stated in Sections 87401 and 87402, to the extent that you participated in the proceedings before the 
Commission. 
A "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" is "any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other detennination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest 
or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative 
agency . . .. " (Section 87400(c) and Regulation 18741.l(a)(3).) It does not include proceedings that 
do not involve specific parties, such as the making of policies or rules of general application. (Black 
Advice Letter, No. A-98-320.) Proceedings to certify or amend a LCP are similar to a pennit or 
licensing proceeding, and are the type of quasi-judicial proceedings covered under sections 87401 
and 87402. 
Previous Participation 
An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the 
proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, fonnal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential 
information . ... " (Section 87400(d).) 


File No. A-17-047 
Page No. 5 
A supervisor is deemed to have participated personally and substantially in any proceeding 
that was pending before the agency under his or her direct supervisory authority. A proceeding is 
under a supervisor's "authority" if the supervisor's duties include direct involvement as follows: 
(A) The supervisor's duties include the primary responsibility within the agency 
for directing the operation or function of the program where the proceeding is 
initiated or conducted. However, this provision does not apply to a supervisor 
who is only responsible for the general oversight of the administrative actions or 
functions of a program in which the responsibilities concerning the specific or 
final review of the proceeding are expressly delegated to other persons in the 
agency. 
(B) The supervisor directly supervises the person performing the investigation, 
review, or other action involved in the proceeding including, but not limited to, 
assigning the matter for which the required conduct is taken. 
(C) The supervisor reviews, discusses, or authorizes any action in the proceeding. 
(D) The supervisor has contact with any of the participants in the proceeding 
regarding the subject of the proceeding. 
(Regulation 18741.l(a)(4).) 
Mere administrative oversight of programs, without reviewing or otherwise directly 
supervising matters involving specific individuals, projects, or decisions under those programs, 
does not rise to the level of personal and substantial involvement implicated by the statute. (In re 
Lucas (2000) 14 FPPC Ops. 14.) 
City of Imperial Beach 
As Executive Director from 2011 to February 2016, you state that you had oversight and 
general direction over the deputy director of the San Diego district office. You do not recall any 
particular involvement in the city's LCP specific amendment proceedings, other than general 
oversight, during this time. Most significantly, for the city's current effort to update its LCP 
pursuant to a planning grant awarded to the city by the Coastal Commission in August of 2016, the 
grant process began and was awarded after you left the agency. You did not participate in the 
review, recommendation or award of the grant prior to leaving office. 
To the extent that your interaction with the city's LCP amendment proceedings was "mere 
administrative oversight of programs, without reviewing or otherwise directly supervising matters 
involving specific individuals, projects, or decisions under those programs" it does not rise to the 
level of "personal and substantial involvement" in the proceedings. (Regulation 18741.1 (a)( 4(A) 
and In re Lucas, supra.) Moreover, as the grant was awarded after you left service, it does not 
appear that you previously participated in the city's current effort to update its LCP. The permanent 
ban does not apply provided that you did not directly supervise the review, recommendation, or 
award of the grant. 


City of Pacific Grove 
File No. A-17-047 
Page No. 6 
In regard the City of Pacific Grove's current effort to certify its LUP and proposed IP, you 
state that your involvement was limited to high-level review of the status of the LCP work with the 
Deputy Director overseeing the city's district. To your knowledge, the Commission stafrs first 
fonnal written comments to the city occurred after you were no longer Executive Director and you 
did not participate in these comments. While the city was awarded its grant to perfonn this work 
while you were Executive Director, you state that your involvement was one of general oversight 
on the implementation of the grant program. 
Again, the "mere administrative oversight of programs, without reviewing or otherwise 
directly supervising matters involving specific individuals, projects, or decisions under those 
programs" does not rise to the level of .. personal and substantial involvement" in the proceedings. 
(Regulation 18741.l(a)(4(A) and 111 re Lucas, supra.) And while the city's grant was awarded while 
you were the executive director, it does not appear that you previously participated in the city's 
current efforts provided that your involvement with the grant program was limited to the general 
oversight on implementation of the program. Under these facts the pennanent ban does not apply. 
However, we caution that this conclusion applies only to the extent that you did not directly 
supervise the review, recommendation, or award of the city's grant application or the city's current 
effort to certify its LUP and proposed IP. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel

Pacific Grove related to updating each city's Local Coastal Program ("LCP")?

participated in more than an oversight role.

sections.analysis:
Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act: the one-year ban and the permanent ban. These provisions 
are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions. (Sections 87400-87406 and 
Regulation 18746.1.)

The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal appearance or any oral or written communication, before his or her former 
agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary

contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1.) 
You are subject to the one-year ban until September 2017 and you may not make a formal or 
informal appearance before the Commission on behalf of a client, for the purpose of influencing the 
amendment or award of an LCP update or certification, during that time. You may provide policy 
review and advice on the proposed LCP to a third party contractor, if you are not identified with 
these efforts and so long as you are not otherwise prohibited from such representation under the 
permanent ban. 


Permanent Ban  
The "pennanent ban" prohibits the representation for compensation of any person other than 
the State of California, in any proceeding you participated in as a public official. Section 87401 
states: 
No fonner state administrative official, after the termination of his or her 
employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, 
or otherwise represent, any other person (other than the State of California) before 
any court or state administrative agency or any officer or employee thereof by 
making any fonnal or infonnal appearance, or by making any oral or written 
communication with the intent to influence, in connection with any judicial, 
quasi-judicial or other proceeding if both of the following apply: 
(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial 
interest. 
(b) The proceeding is one in which the fonner state administrative official 
participated. 
Section 87402 applies this ban to assisting others in such representation: 
No fonner state administrative official, after the tennination of his or her 
employment or tenn of office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel, consult 
or assist in representing any other person (except the State of California) in any 
proceeding in which the official would be prohibited from appearing under 
Section 87401 
As the former Executive Director of the Commission, you are subject to the pennanent ban 
stated in Sections 87401 and 87402, to the extent that you participated in the proceedings before the 
Commission. 
A "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" is "any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other detennination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest 
or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative 
agency . . .. " (Section 87400(c) and Regulation 18741.l(a)(3).) It does not include proceedings that 
do not involve specific parties, such as the making of policies or rules of general application. (Black 
Advice Letter, No. A-98-320.) Proceedings to certify or amend a LCP are similar to a pennit or 
licensing proceeding, and are the type of quasi-judicial proceedings covered under sections 87401 
and 87402. 
Previous Participation 
An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the 
proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, fonnal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential 
information . ... " (Section 87400(d).)  
A supervisor is deemed to have participated personally and substantially in any proceeding 
that was pending before the agency under his or her direct supervisory authority. A proceeding is

(A) The supervisor's duties include the primary responsibility within the agency 
for directing the operation or function of the program where the proceeding is 
initiated or conducted. However, this provision does not apply to a supervisor 
who is only responsible for the general oversight of the administrative actions or 
functions of a program in which the responsibilities concerning the specific or 
final review of the proceeding are expressly delegated to other persons in the 
agency. 
(B) The supervisor directly supervises the person performing the investigation, 
review, or other action involved in the proceeding including, but not limited to, 
assigning the matter for which the required conduct is taken. 
(C) The supervisor reviews, discusses, or authorizes any action in the proceeding. 
(D) The supervisor has contact with any of the participants in the proceeding 
regarding the subject of the proceeding. 
(Regulation 18741.l(a)(4).) 
Mere administrative oversight of programs, without reviewing or otherwise directly 
supervising matters involving specific individuals, projects, or decisions under those programs,

City of Imperial Beach 
As Executive Director from 2011 to February 2016, you state that you had oversight and 
general direction over the deputy director of the San Diego district office. You do not recall any

pursuant to a planning grant awarded to the city by the Coastal Commission in August of 2016, the

review, recommendation or award of the grant prior to leaving office. 
To the extent that your interaction with the city's LCP amendment proceedings was "mere 
administrative oversight of programs, without reviewing or otherwise directly supervising matters 
involving specific individuals, projects, or decisions under those programs" it does not rise to the 
level of "personal and substantial involvement" in the proceedings. (Regulation 18741.1 (a)( 4(A) 
and In re Lucas, supra.) Moreover, as the grant was awarded after you left service, it does not

City of Pacific Grove  
In regard the City of Pacific Grove's current effort to certify its LUP and proposed IP, you

while you were Executive Director, you state that your involvement was one of general oversight
